sentences.text: Klaus Ollendorff.
K-L-A-U-S O-L-L-E-N-D-O-R-F-F as in Frank.
It's the same.
No.
I'm going to be, I'm ashamed to say it, 79 in June.
Breslau.
B as in boy, R-E-S as in Sam, L-A-U.
It's in Germany, and it is the capital of Silesia, which is a province of Prussia.
The city, little, for early recollections-- recollections.

My main recollections are my father bringing a rocking horse to me when I was about three, on which I sat and sang loudly, mostly military songs, and my mother taking me to the garden and let me cut flowers.
And a major event in my life was when I got a new governess.
The old one had left.
I do not remember her.
And shortly before my fourth birthday, Fraulein Gustel, means Miss Gustel was her name, G-U-S-T-E-L, came, and it was love at first sight.
And she stayed with me until I was 12 years old, which may have been my ruin because I was over indulged and I didn't have to do anything.
When I started cutting paper dolls, she said, oh no, it won't do.
I'll do it for you.
And she made a profound impression on me.
She was a devout Catholic.
And she took me to church quite often.
My parents had no objection to that.
And-- And she always told me about her life, which was in the forester's house on the Rothschild estate in Bohemia.
Her father was a forester.
And when she told me about her childhood, I always visualized that house in the forest, and I expected Hansel and Gretel to come any minute.
It was very romantic.
And when I was much, much older, like almost 50, I went to Czechoslovakia with my older son, and we visited her.
And we saw the house and the forest, and it wasn't quite as mysterious and romantic as I had pictured it.
But I was very glad to have been there.
I remember the day she left our employ, which was May 8, 1931.
She-- And we took her to the railroad station.
And I tried to suppress my tears as much as I could.
But I have the feeling I wasn't too successful.
She came back to visit next year for my bar mitzvah.
In other words, I was nearly 12 when she left, but she came for the bar mitzvah.
And that she was the most important person for me at the bar mitzvah, with the exception of one other person whom I will bring up later.
Coming back to my early childhood, I did not experience anti-Semitism until when I was five years old and I was walking with my governess.
I was bouncing a ball, and the ball rolled ahead of me.
And I ran after to retrieve it.
And a man saw me pick up the ball and said to me, what do you want, you little Jewboy?
And so I didn't even know what it all meant.
And when I came home, I told my father about it.
And my father says, well, this man was an anti-Semite.
And so I said, what does all this mean?
And so that was the first time really that I heard about or experienced anti-Semitism.
And when I started school, when I was little under 6-- the school year started in April.
I was unaware of any anti-Semitism.
And the teacher I had was a Lutheran who observed Martin's Day, which was Martin Luther's Day.
And on that day, certain sweet rolls were brought to school.
I think his wife baked them.
And I've always looked forward to Martin's Day.
And he told us about Martin Luther.
And I was always quite impressed by Martin Luther because he put it in the context of Luther being opposed to the great taxation by the Catholic church.
But that confused me because I really liked Catholics better.
My governess was Catholic.
And later on in life, my Christian friends were Catholics.
What else I want to say about the first grade was that there was-- it was-- it was 1923, maybe 1924.
There was runaway inflation in Germany.
There was enormous poverty.
And the American Quakers came to school, to our school every day, to dispense hot chocolate.
And the-- this-- people had to be on a certain list for this.
I could not be because one had to prove-- what is that called-- need--
Proof.
[INAUDIBLE] need.
And-- but I tasted it once and it was delicious.
And I was very grateful to the Quakers, and so were the other kids.
And that procedure was-- became a-- a verb.
It was called Quaken.
You know, when-- when people drank that hot chocolate, they Quakered.
We celebrated Christmas in the school.
And we had creches.
And it smelled wonderful, the burning pine.
And we sang songs.
And I sang loudly and always attempted to sing second voice to make for better harmonies.
I don't know whether I succeeded or I did not.
Now I cannot do it for sure.
Yes.
Yes, I had a sister.
Have-- still have her.
She was born three years before me and--
Her name is Ursula.
And-- married name is Ursula, then-- And my mother was really a mother and housewife even though she was a rather good photographer.
She had studied photography before she got married for maybe three or four months.
But it was enough to make her a pretty good photographer.
And my father had inherited a business from my grandfather whose name was Jakob Ollendorff.
And Jakob Ollendorff lived in Kattowitz-- Kattowitz, which is in Upper Silesia, which after World War I became Polish, was called Katowice.
But before World War I already, my grandfather had moved his main office to Breslau.
He had an interest in a couple of coal mines, not a majority interest.
It was just a little interest.
But that little interest enabled him to buy, to become a coal distributor.
And he sold coal by the carloads to industries.
I-- I remember later on when there was a coal strike and when miners struck in England, my grandfather, of course, was dead by the time.
My father had an enormous amount of business because people would buy coal, you know, from wherever they could scrape it up.
But now coming back to my house-- yeah, my grandfather had a wife.
Her name was Jenny Ollendorff, who was one of I think 11 or 13 children, I'm not quite sure.
And he, my grandfather, had been a consul or vice-consul for Bolivia, I believe.
And I remember photographs and pictures when I went to my grandmother's house which showed my grandfather's house displaying the German flag and the Bolivian flag on certain holidays.
My father worked very hard.
He went to his office six days a week.
And he did come home for lunch, which was really the dinner.
He always came around 1:45.
We ate for half an hour.
Then he took a 15 minute nap.
And then he went back to work.
He set a tremendous example for me, but it boomeranged.
I-- I never followed in his-- I never adopted his work habits.
My mother took a one hour nap every day.
And I always looked forward to her waking up because then we could play or do something, anything but homework.
Well, I had violin lessons, and she played piano moderately well.
And accompanied me and was not very critical of me and-- Very often said, when I squeaked a little less, this is beautiful.
And she was great.
And I went out of the house too, and I played in the garden.
Kids dropped in.
Friends of mine dropped in, and they loved to come to our house because my mother was very nice to them, unusually nice to all of them.
And I had one friend-- and I now go to the year 1934-- I had one friend who was a Galician immigrant who didn't look clean.
And my father just couldn't stand him, and he didn't want him to come to the house.
And the kid picked that up, my father's dislike for him.
One day when he was at our house, he cut himself on something.
And his finger began to bleed.
And my mother called, saw that and said, come.
And she took him to her room and washed the hand and administered to the finger.

And afterward, he said to me, it was so nice of your mother to be so accepting of me, which is just terrible and which points to the great prejudice, unjustified prejudice, which many German Jews had to its eastern Jews.
And that was those who came from territories east of the Vistula River.
In America, fathers always try to be in touch with their children's feelings.
I always tried to be in touch with my father's feelings.
I was afraid of him, and-- but I know under it that all he loved me a lot.
He was interested in everything I did, overly interested.
And-- and there was no doubt that he was the master of the house.
We celebrated the high holidays, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, and that was always celebrated at my father's mother's house, my grandmother.
And--
Jenny Ollendorff, Jenny, Jenny Ollendorff.
She was a name Marck, M-A-R-C-K.
And we went to temple, and my father belonged to a reform temple but not so reform that we did not cover their heads.
In fact, they all-- the men always wore top hats.
And so I would usually stand next to my father for two hours, share the seat with him.
He had a permanent seat in the fourth row, I believe.
And I always looked forward when he-- was glad when he turned pages because it would soon be over.
My mother occasionally came too, they-- She went upstairs because women were separated.
And she looked down, sometimes waved to me.
And I could tell from her face that she also couldn't wait for the service to be over.
Yes.
When she was young for sure.
And when she was older, she refused and probably went to some youth service where she might meet some boys.
And but we also celebrated Christmas.
And we had a Christmas tree.
And we sang Christmas songs.
And later on we also celebrated Hanukkah.
We played with the dreidel.
And then there was a little gambling game, which I forgot, with cards, which I always enjoyed royally.
Where did you spend Passover?
At my grandmother's also.
And who else was there?
Did you have cousins in the city?
Yes.
Yes, they-- some-- I had many second cousins in the city.
In other words, my father's first cousins.
And quite often several of them were invited to those Seders.
And my father's brother would often conduct that Seder.
And I enjoyed it because it was an abbreviated Seder, and my uncle did it with quite a bit of humor.
And it did make me aware of the plight of the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I don't know.
I don't know whether it's common for to tell a joke during an interview like this.

But when Moses went up the mountain to talk to God, to find out what the commandments will be, he was gone for a whole day, and his flock was standing at the bottom of the mountain, waiting anxiously.
And finally Moses came down with the two tablets.
And he said, I'll give you the good news first.
I got him down to 10.
The bad news is-- what is infidelity?
Adultery is in.
And I can't remember too well, of our-- too much of our religious life.
It was--
Jews and Gentiles.
And in fact, my mother had a friend whose son was a good friend of mine, who was a Nazi.
But she was a very good friend of my mother's.
And on the day of the boycott day against Jewish stores, you know, when Jews were supposed to have been avoided, this lady came to visit my mother but kept her Nazi insignia buttons on her coat.
No, my mother told me about it.

I became aware of it in 1932 when some of my friends-- there were hardly any Jews in my class at that time-- when some of my friends told me-- who belonged to the Hitler Youth-- told me that they learned what kind of methods to use to make people confess.
And one, my best friend told me, that they experimented with dropping water from an eyedropper into people's ears to see how soon they become disoriented and dizzy.
And I said to him, do they teach other things like this too?
He said, yes, lots of things.
And-- but I didn't take it very seriously.
But in 1933-- 4-- 1934, that same friend said to me, you know, you better get out of Germany.
It's going to be terrible for Jews.
And when I came home and I told that to my father, he said, not to us, it won't be, you know.
I was a veteran from the war.
I have the Iron Cross.
My brother has the Iron Cross.
I always voted conservative German-- they won't to do anything to me.
And I said, you know, you don't hear the bad stuff because everybody kowtows to you.
When he walks down the street, people bow.
Nobody would tell him what the-- what the general feeling is.
Of course, at that time, it was only a feeling.
Because the Wannsee Conference, you know, which made the decision to exterminate, where the decision to exterminate the Jews was formalized, happened only many years later.
So I can understand my father for not wanting to leave this cushioned life because he didn't suffer from this anti-Semitism.
I didn't suffer either, but I heard about it.
Yes.
Yeah, we had-- we had--
Well, we had at the height of-- of the period when we had the most servants, we had a cook, a maid, a butler, the governess, a chauffeur who was split with my uncle.
I mean, he didn't live in the house because we didn't-- the car was kept in a garage someplace.
And-- and then we had a little apartment in the basement which was rented out rent-free to a couple who in return for this rent-free apartment took care of the furnace and other janitorial duties.
No.
No, next to us was a countess.
Countess von Puckler.
They were very, very aristocratic and-- and I think relatively pleasant.
I didn't talk much to them.
I walked to school until I started gymnasium.
Then I rode the streetcar.
And later on, I rode my bike.
No.
No, I would have been embarrassed.
And it wasn't offered either.
He drove my father.
Not in the morning.
In the morning, my father took the street car.
When he came home for lunch, the chauffeur drove him.
Waited and then drove him back.
Yes.
The cousin's name was Dorthe, which was Dorothy.
And she was five years older than I.
And I was very fond of her.
And I thought she was a great style setter.
And I got to know her much better, much, much better later after she moved to Chicago and I moved to Chicago.
She had gone to Palestine--
And then she got married there in 1934.
And then 1937-- she had a child then in 1935, and then in 1937 she came to Chicago.
And ultimately she got divorced.
This man she married in Palestine deserted her and ran away with an older woman, much older woman.
And my cousin survived this ordeal.
And two years later married the very old man whose wife had deserted him in order-- [LAUGHTER] in order to marry my cousin's husband.
So it was an exchange.
Yes.
Oh, I prepared a lot because everyone had to pass an-- a test, which was given in the rabbi's study.
And I had to read the haftarah.
The most difficult part was it showed no vowels because I had to read from the Torah.
So I studied in the book with vowels.
And a week or two before the bar mitzvah, I had to go to the rabbi's study, and he tested me on the contents of this particular chapter.
I forgot what it was.
And he was very strict.
And I was very scared.
But I passed, and I got a certificate that I had passed.
And-- and I was very glad when the bar mitzvah was over.
Yeah--
Oh, yes, we had-- There was a luncheon after the celebration, and I remember we had cold salmon, which was delicious.
And I was very, very tired because I hadn't slept the night before.
And I was tired throughout the day.
And during the dinner, I got even more tired.
I felt like falling asleep.
But I do remember that a second cousin of my father's, her name was Paula Ollendorff, who was a prominent social worker, gave a speech.
She had just come back from-- from the United States where she had worked with Jane Addams.
You know this social worker?
The mother of social worker?
And she had been in Washington to see certain politicians.
And she said that Germany owes a great debt to President Hoover who has-- who at this very moment is approaching Congress with a request for funds to assist Germany.
And these funds will avert a takeover by the radical right and the radical left.
That was her prediction.
I often think of this.
You know, when we give foreign aid now, I always think, you know, what a good investment it really is.
And if Germany could have been saved from Hitler, if they had gotten some more loan money.
Because the economy was on the rocks.
And during the-- there were two elections in 1932 prior to Hitler's being appointed chancellor.
And I think you could hear many versions of this.
But that is the way I remember it.
